children.title: Raspberry Pi power-on behavior without OS

children.value: Raspberry Pi power-on behavior without OS

children.description: More options for Raspberry Pi power-on behavior without OS

children.value: if i plug in rasp pi will it blink w/out a boot os

children.title: Recalled diagnostic LED behavior for unpowered Raspberry Pi

children.value: Recalled diagnostic LED behavior for unpowered Raspberry Pi

yeah. red pwr led goes solid the moment it gets power, that's hardwired. the green act led will also blink, but as a diagnostic code rather than normal activity. with no sd card or no bootable os on it, recent pis (including the zero 2 w) flash the green led in patterns like 3, 4, or 7 blinks to signal things like "no start.elf" or "no kernel image." so yes, you'll see lights, just not the steady healthy blink you get once an os is actually booting.
hmm... no red led is turning on, how to diagnose?
no red led at all usually means it's not getting power, not a boot problem. order of things to check:

the power source itself is the usual culprit. a lot of usb-c cables are charge-only or can't deliver enough current, and phone chargers often sag under the pi's draw. swap to a known-good cable and a proper 5v supply (official pi psu or a solid 3a+ usb-c brick). if you've got a usb power meter lying around, that tells you instantly whether current is flowing.
then the port. zero 2 w has two usb-c ports labeled pwr in and usb. make sure you're in pwr in, not the data one.

if power and cable check out, look at the board itself under decent light. check for anything obvious near the power input: a dislodged fuse, scorched component, bent pin, solder bridge, debris in the usb-c port. the pwr led is tied directly to the 5v rail through a small resistor, so if 5v isn't reaching the board or the rail is shorted, it stays dark.
try it bare: no sd card, no hat, no peripherals, nothing on the gpio. sometimes a hat or a shorted gpio pin pulls the rail down hard enough that the led never lights.

if you've got a multimeter, measure across the test pads (tp1 and tp2 on most pi models) with power applied. should read ~5v. if it's 0, power isn't reaching the board. if it's low (like 3v), something downstream is pulling it down.
what psu and cable are you using?
would it be dangerous to try a mac charger block for this

no, not dangerous. a mac charger is actually overkill in a good way. usb-c pd chargers negotiate voltage with the device, so a 30w, 67w, or 96w mac brick will just deliver 5v at whatever current the pi asks for (the pi zero 2 w pulls well under 1a typically). you can't "force" more power into a device than it draws.

the only real caveat is the cable. a lot of usb-c to usb-c cables that ship with laptops are e-marked for high wattage but still fine for 5v. more common failure mode is a cheap cable that just doesn't carry power well or is data-only. if the mac brick + cable combo gives you nothing, swap the cable before blaming the brick.
one footnote: some very early usb-c pi 4 units had a cc resistor bug where they wouldn't negotiate with e-marked cables, but the zero 2 w doesn't have that issue.
where is the red led supposed to be? i plugged it in (via a usb c to micro adapter) and don't see anything

there's your problem, almost certainly. the pi zero 2 w uses micro-usb for power, not usb-c, so a usb-c to micro adapter should in theory work, but these adapters are notoriously flaky. many of them don't pass enough current, or the cc pin configuration on the usb-c side prevents the charger from supplying 5v at all (usb-c needs proper cc resistors to enable power; adapters often get this wrong). a mac brick especially will refuse to output anything if it doesn't see a valid cc signal.

skip the adapter. use a direct usb-a to micro-usb cable plugged into any 5v usb-a source (old phone charger, laptop usb port, usb-a port on a power brick). that's the setup the zero 2 w is designed for and it sidesteps all the usb-c negotiation weirdness.

as for where the led is: on the zero 2 w there's a single green act led on the top side of the board, near the micro-sd slot on the opposite edge from the usb ports. the zero 2 w doesn't actually have a separate red pwr led like the bigger pis do, just the one green one. so "no red led" is partly expected, the thing to look for is the green one lighting up or blinking when power hits.
do i need the sd card to have anything to see the light

no. the green led will light up briefly the moment power reaches the board, regardless of whether there's an sd card in it. with no card (or an unbootable card) you'll typically see it flash on for a second or two and then either stay off or blink a diagnostic pattern. with a valid boot card you'll see it flicker actively during boot and then pulse with disk activity.
so if you're seeing nothing at all with a known-good power source, it's still a power delivery issue, not a boot issue. the adapter is still my prime suspect.
hmm i dont have a normal micro usb cable though?
a few options depending on what you have around:

easiest is probably an old android phone charger or e-reader cable. anything pre-2017ish android, kindle, bluetooth headphones, portable battery packs, ps4/xbox one controllers all use micro-usb. check a junk drawer, you almost certainly have one. amazon/target/any drugstore has them for a couple bucks if not.
otherwise, if you want to make the usb-c adapter route work, the issue is specifically the cc resistor situation. a usb-c to micro-usb

(not an adapter on the end of a c-to-c cable) from a reputable brand is more likely to have the cc pins configured correctly than a little dongle adapter. but this is still less reliable than just using native micro-usb.

one thing worth trying with what you have now: plug the usb-c end into a computer's usb-c port or a usb-a-to-c cable into a wall usb-a port, rather than a pd wall brick. usb-a sources just dumbly output 5v without any negotiation, so they bypass the cc resistor problem entirely. if your adapter chain ends in usb-c going into a mac brick, that's the worst case for negotiation failure.
